content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>

<html:p>I occasionally give presentations to undergraduates, and one of my favourites is taking the students on a journey of optimising a "binary to decimal" routine<html:sup id="fnref:yeah"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:yeah">1</html:a></html:sup>. There are a number of tricks, which I won't go in to here, but the opening question I have is "how do you even turn a number into its ASCII representation?"</html:p>

<html:p>If you've never stopped to think about it, take a moment now to do so, it can be a fun problem.</html:p>

<html:p>The simple<html:sup id="fnref:naive"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:naive">2</html:a></html:sup> approach is to use <html:code>number % 10</html:code> to get the rightmost digit (adding 48 to turn it into the ASCII number), then divide by ten, and keep going until the number is zero. This produces the digits backwards<html:sup id="fnref:bit"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:bit">3</html:a></html:sup>, but you can reverse them afterwards, which I won't show here. This routine is one of the few legitimate uses of <html:code>do while</html:code> in C, as we always want to emit at least one digit even if the number <html:em>is</html:em> zero to start with. The code looks something like:</html:p>

<html:p>Here the compiler does a fantastic job. <html:a href="/202512/06-dividing-to-conquer">Yesterday</html:a> we saw how division by powers of two can be optimised to shifts; today we'll see the compiler manages to avoid expensive division even when we <html:em>aren't</html:em> dividing by a power of two. It also gets the remainder cheaply, which we usually get for free from the divide instruction.</html:p>

<html:p>The transformation is quite clever - let's walk through the annotated assembly:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nf">to_decimal_backwards</html:span><html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="no">unsigned</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">int</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">char</html:span><html:span class="p">*):</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rax = buf</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">esi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">3435973837</html:span><html:span class="w">       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; esi = 0xcccccccd</html:span>
<html:span class="nl">.L2:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; edx = number</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ecx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ecx = number</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">1</html:span><html:span class="w">                </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ++buf</html:span>

<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">imul</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rsi</html:span><html:span class="w">             </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rdx *= 0xcccccccd</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">shr</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">35</html:span><html:span class="w">               </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rdx = rdx >> 35</html:span>
<html:span class="w">                            </html:span><html:span class="c1">; rdx = number / 10 [see below]</html:span>

<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">lea</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdx</html:span><html:span class="err">+</html:span><html:span class="no">rdx</html:span><html:span class="p">*</html:span><html:span class="mi">4</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">      </html:span><html:span class="c1">; r8 = rdx * 5</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; r8 = rdx * 5 * 2 = rdx * 10</html:span>
<html:span class="w">                            </html:span><html:span class="c1">; r8 = (number / 10) * 10 [rounded down]</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">sub</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ecx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">r8d</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ecx = number - (number / 10) * 10</html:span>
<html:span class="w">                            </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ecx = number % 10</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ecx</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">48</html:span><html:span class="w">               </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ecx = '0' + (number % 10)</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">9</html:span><html:span class="w">                </html:span><html:span class="c1">; number > 9?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">edx</html:span><html:span class="w">              </html:span><html:span class="c1">; number = number / 10</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">mov</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">BYTE</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rax-1</html:span><html:span class="p">],</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">cl</html:span><html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; *(buf-1) = '0' + (number % 10)</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ja</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L2</html:span><html:span class="w">                    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; loop if number (prior to divide) >= 10</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">ret</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>There's a lot to unpack here, several different optimisations, but the main one is how the compiler has turned division by a constant ten into a multiply and a shift. There's a magic constant <html:code>0xcccccccd</html:code> and a shift right of 35! Shifting right by 35 is the same as dividing by 2<html:sup>35</html:sup> - what's going on?<html:sup id="fnref:imul"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:imul">4</html:a></html:sup></html:p>

<html:p>Let's see what happens each step of the algorithm:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="o">>>></html:span> <html:span class="mi">1234</html:span> <html:span class="o">*</html:span> <html:span class="mh">0xcccccccd</html:span>
<html:span class="mi">4239991714858</html:span>
<html:span class="o">>>></html:span> <html:span class="mi">4239991714858</html:span> <html:span class="o">//</html:span> <html:span class="p">(</html:span><html:span class="mi">2</html:span><html:span class="o">**</html:span><html:span class="mi">35</html:span><html:span class="p">)</html:span>
<html:span class="mi">123</html:span>
<html:span class="o">>>></html:span> <html:span class="mi">123</html:span> <html:span class="o">*</html:span> <html:span class="mi">10</html:span>
<html:span class="mi">1230</html:span>
<html:span class="o">>>></html:span> <html:span class="mi">1234</html:span> <html:span class="o">-</html:span> <html:span class="mi">1230</html:span>
<html:span class="mi">4</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>What's happening is that <html:code>0xcccccccd / 2**35</html:code> is very close to ⅒ (around 0.10000000000582077). By multiplying our input value by this constant first, then shifting right, we're doing fixed-point multiplication by ⅒ - which is division by ten. The compiler knows that for all possible unsigned integer values, this trick will always give the right answer. For other values and signednesses, sometimes it needs to account for rounding, for example, dividing a signed value by three:</html:p>

<html:p>Here we see that it has to account for rounding. If you edit the code above and try dividing by fifteen, you'll see that causes even more code to be emitted. However, it's all still faster than a real divide instruction.</html:p>

<html:p>Back to our ASCII conversion example, to get the remainder (the modulus); the compiler takes the (truncated) <html:code>number / 10</html:code>, multiplies it back up by 10 using <html:code>lea</html:code> tricks (we've covered this <html:a href="/202512/02-adding-integers">before</html:a>), and then the difference between the original number and this computed value is the remainder.</html:p>

<html:p>The rest of the optimisations are the compiler trying to do work eagerly (like incrementing <html:code>buf</html:code>), and checking one loop iteration ahead: there's no point looping if the current number is less than or equal to 9.</html:p>

<html:p>Overall, some very clever optimisations that avoid division entirely!</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>See <html:a href="https://youtu.be/V9Pvv1tkocM">the video</html:a> that accompanies this post.</html:em></html:p>

<html:hr />

<html:p><html:em>This post is day 7 of <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025</html:a>,
a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>← <html:a href="/202512/06-dividing-to-conquer">Division</html:a> | <html:a href="/202512/08-going-loopy">Going loopy</html:a> →</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>Support Compiler Explorer on <html:a href="https://patreon.com/c/mattgodbolt">Patreon</html:a>
or <html:a href="https://github.com/sponsors/compiler-explorer">GitHub</html:a>,
or by buying CE products in the <html:a href="https://shop.compiler-explorer.com">Compiler Explorer Shop</html:a></html:em>.</html:p>

<html:div class="footnote">
<html:hr />
<html:ol>
<html:li id="fn:yeah">
<html:p>A decade ago many trading systems still used <html:a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_Information_eXchange">FIX</html:a> to accept new orders, and that's essentially ASCII. Taking prices and quantities and quickly turning them into ASCII used to be important! <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:yeah" title="Jump back to footnote 1 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:naive">
<html:p>Yes I know I said I wouldn't go into it, but the faster implementation I wrote uses bit trickery to get log(10) of the number, then dispatches via template trickery to a "N-digit" routine that then uses a 100-entry lookup table to look up pairs of digits. Fun stuff. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:naive" title="Jump back to footnote 2 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:bit">
<html:p>If you can get the log(10) you can get the number of digits in the output, and then you can start at the right spot in the output buffer and write the number backwards. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:bit" title="Jump back to footnote 3 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:imul">
<html:p>You might wonder why the compiler uses <html:code>imul</html:code> (signed multiply) even though we're working with unsigned values. The low 64 bits of the result are identical for both signed and unsigned multiplication, and <html:code>imul</html:code> has more flexible operand forms that let us specify three operands. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:imul" title="Jump back to footnote 4 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
</html:ol>
</html:div>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. I occasionally give presentations to undergraduates, and one of my favourites is taking the students on a journey of optimising a "binary to decimal" routine 1 . There are a number of tricks, which I won't go in to here, but the opening question I have is "how do you even turn a number into its ASCII representation?" If you've never stopped to think about it, take a moment now to do so, it can be a fun problem. The simple 2 approach is to use number % 10 to get the rightmost digit (adding 48 to turn it into the ASCII number), then divide by ten, and keep going until the number is zero. This produces the digits backwards 3 , but you can reverse them afterwards, which I won't show here. This routine is one of the few legitimate uses of do while in C, as we always want to emit at least one digit even if the number is zero to start with. The code looks something like: Here the compiler does a fantastic job. Yesterday we saw how division by powers of two can be optimised to shifts; today we'll see the compiler manages to avoid expensive division even when we aren't dividing by a power of two. It also gets the remainder cheaply, which we usually get for free from the divide instruction. The transformation is quite clever - let's walk through the annotated assembly: to_decimal_backwards ( unsigned int , char *): mov rax , rsi ; rax = buf mov esi , 3435973837 ; esi = 0xcccccccd .L2: mov edx , edi ; edx = number mov ecx , edi ; ecx = number add rax , 1 ; ++buf imul rdx , rsi ; rdx *= 0xcccccccd shr rdx , 35 ; rdx = rdx >> 35 ; rdx = number / 10 [see below] lea r8d , [ rdx + rdx * 4 ] ; r8 = rdx * 5 add r8d , r8d ; r8 = rdx * 5 * 2 = rdx * 10 ; r8 = (number / 10) * 10 [rounded down] sub ecx , r8d ; ecx = number - (number / 10) * 10 ; ecx = number % 10 add ecx , 48 ; ecx = '0' + (number % 10) cmp edi , 9 ; number > 9? mov edi , edx ; number = number / 10 mov BYTE PTR [ rax-1 ], cl ; *(buf-1) = '0' + (number % 10) ja .L2 ; loop if number (prior to divide) >= 10 ret There's a lot to unpack here, several different optimisations, but the main one is how the compiler has turned division by a constant ten into a multiply and a shift. There's a magic constant 0xcccccccd and a shift right of 35! Shifting right by 35 is the same as dividing by 2 35 - what's going on? 4 Let's see what happens each step of the algorithm: >>> 1234 * 0xcccccccd 4239991714858 >>> 4239991714858 // ( 2 ** 35 ) 123 >>> 123 * 10 1230 >>> 1234 - 1230 4 What's happening is that 0xcccccccd / 2**35 is very close to ⅒ (around 0.10000000000582077). By multiplying our input value by this constant first, then shifting right, we're doing fixed-point multiplication by ⅒ - which is division by ten. The compiler knows that for all possible unsigned integer values, this trick will always give the right answer. For other values and signednesses, sometimes it needs to account for rounding, for example, dividing a signed value by three: Here we see that it has to account for rounding. If you edit the code above and try dividing by fifteen, you'll see that causes even more code to be emitted. However, it's all still faster than a real divide instruction. Back to our ASCII conversion example, to get the remainder (the modulus); the compiler takes the (truncated) number / 10 , multiplies it back up by 10 using lea tricks (we've covered this before ), and then the difference between the original number and this computed value is the remainder. The rest of the optimisations are the compiler trying to do work eagerly (like incrementing buf ), and checking one loop iteration ahead: there's no point looping if the current number is less than or equal to 9. Overall, some very clever optimisations that avoid division entirely! See the video that accompanies this post. This post is day 7 of Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025 , a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code. ← Division | Going loopy → This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans. Support Compiler Explorer on Patreon or GitHub , or by buying CE products in the Compiler Explorer Shop . A decade ago many trading systems still used FIX to accept new orders, and that's essentially ASCII. Taking prices and quantities and quickly turning them into ASCII used to be important! ↩ Yes I know I said I wouldn't go into it, but the faster implementation I wrote uses bit trickery to get log(10) of the number, then dispatches via template trickery to a "N-digit" routine that then uses a 100-entry lookup table to look up pairs of digits. Fun stuff. ↩ If you can get the log(10) you can get the number of digits in the output, and then you can start at the right spot in the output buffer and write the number backwards. ↩ You might wonder why the compiler uses imul (signed multiply) even though we're working with unsigned values. The low 64 bits of the result are identical for both signed and unsigned multiplication, and imul has more flexible operand forms that let us specify three operands. ↩
summary: How compilers avoid expensive division with multiplication tricks
title: Multiplying our way out of division
